65 - Jeremy Miner Breaks Down Jordan Belfort SELLING Live - YouTube.mp4
Hey, it's Jeremy Miner, founder of 7th Level. Now, in this training video, I'm going to break down the wolf of Wall Street, Jordan Belfort's, one of his sales calls. I don't know if it's a live one or if it's— role play— but I'm going to break down everything he does. I'm going to show you the techniques he's using so you can spot it. Then I'm going to show you some tweaks that I would personally make myself. Okay. Now. If you're unfamiliar with who I am, I had an 18-year sales career. I sold in four completely different industries, two business-to-consumer and two business-to-business. I made a bit under $33 million in straight commissions in those four industries in that close to 18-year sales career. But who cares about me?
Let's go to the training video. I hope you enjoy this. Thank you. Good night. Hey. Now, why didn't you ring me up earlier? Nice to meet you. Thanks for taking the meeting. Thanks for coming in. Great. Have a good day. So far, so good. Yeah. So far, so good. Sorry, Dennis, I've only got like 20 minutes. So I want to be playful. So when they walk in, okay, it doesn't matter if they're the biggest CEO of the Fortune 100 company. I did this all the time when I sold at the enterprise level. They'd walk in like, 'Hey, Jeremy Miner.' Hey, Jeremy Miner, I looked you guys up online. It says mainly good things about your company. And I'll be all playful. And they'd be like, 'Oh.' And they would just laugh.
Their guard literally comes down. Now, you can't—like— be in a serious tone, like, 'Hey, Jeremy Miner, I looked up your company and it's mainly good things about it.' That's going to be weird and they're going to get defensive. You have to use that playful tone. So a little tweak there. Hey, Jeremy Miner. Hey, Jeremy Miner. I know we have about 20 minutes or so. I did look you up blind. It mainly said good things about you. And I'm just playful. Okay, and just what happens, I'm not trying to be a class clown, but the ultimate goal is to get the prospect to let their guard down. Okay, it's just a disarming technique. When the prospect lacks... It releases dopamine in their brain, okay? Causes them to laugh.
And now they're a little bit, slowly but surely, they let their guard down. Just a little tweak I would do. Jordan does a great job here. Just a little tweak I would do, okay? Thanks for coming in. Great. Have a good day. So far, so good. Yeah. So far, so good. Sorry, Dennis, I've only got like 20 minutes. Now, another tweak I would do instead of saying having a good day or how are you today? A lot of salespeople say that and that's fine. I typically want to say something different because if I say this. same thing that every prospect is used to most salespeople saying like, how are you doing today? Or how's your day? Or are you having a good day? A lot of times, unless they're like an easier prospect.
especially if they're A-type. They're used to salespeople saying that. So you're instantly, they're associating you with all the other salespeople that they don't like, okay? You're triggering fight or flight in their- brain by using the same verbiage. So I might just re-language it. You can say the same thing, but you re-language it because they're not used to that script. They're not used to that pattern. I might say, 'So what's going on in your neck of the woods today?' 'So what's going on in your neck of the woods today?' Is there any trouble you're creating or what's going on? And I'm just playful. It's a different way. Ask the same question, but the prospect's not used to that verbiage. Okay. So I'm not triggering any guard coming up. Okay.
Let's keep going here. So real quick, I mean, Now, another thing I know I'm being picky here. I love Jordan. I'm just always analyzing everything here. So another thing that I would change when the prospect says, 'Hey, I've only got 20. 20 minutes.' I can't remember what he said. He's like, 'Hey, I only got 20 minutes.' So hopefully, when we get to the point— or something like that— what that means is the prospect is showing you or her a thought. Like, they're framing you now, subconsciously, consciously, they don't know they're doing that. They're just showing you, like, you're a salesperson. I don't have a lot of time. Get to the point. They're basically saying that. So you don't want to just jump in and start selling or talking or, oh, I appreciate it.
Yes, I'll be quick. So thankful for to be here in the meeting, which. Jordan didn't do that, obviously, but you never want to do that. I instantly want to take that frame back. So I might look down at my watch. Oh, geez, you guys must have a lot of time on your hands today of 20 minutes. I've got maybe 10, 15 max before I've got to get my Uber to my next appointment. Should we go and get started? Just something like that. I'm playful and then I'm serious. It raises my status. It's called situational status and social dynamics, okay? Oh, geez, you must hear my tone, the playful tone. Oh, goodness, you must have a lot of time on your hands around here. I've got maybe 10, 12 minutes before my next appointment.
Should we go and get started? And bam, you're right there. I've got maybe 10 or 12 minutes before I've got to get my driver to pick me up for the next appointment. Should we go ahead and get started? And instantly you're going to see the guard come down from the prospect and they're going to view you as like, oh. They must be, they've got other people they're talking to. They're busy. It's not something a salesperson would ever do. And it actually raises your status, showing that you have equal status to this prospect that you're just as busy as well. Because if you go in and say, 'Oh yeah,' I'll be quick and whatever, all that does is you're signaling to the prospect that their time should be respected.
But your time as a salesperson should not be respected, and that lowers your status in their brain. That's not something you want to happen. Okay, let's keep going here. This is going to be interesting. Thank you. I guess the way I usually work is I kind of want to know about you, your company, what kind of challenges you're facing right now, what's right in your business, what's wrong in your business. And based on that, what we do here at LMK is we kind of tailor-make our strategies directly to suit our clients' needs. We have three areas of excellence we focus on. Mm-hmm. Capital markets, hedging strategies, commodities hedging, finance for your business. B, investment banking, meaning if you're looking to go public, raise any money for specific projects, both foreign and domestic.
And lastly, of course, asset management, your land, your trucks, and trying to maximize your value there. But again, that's just broad areas. I look to each client kind of as a unique situation. We try to kind of tailor it. If you had to say one thing right now in your business that was kind of like giving you the most. Now, just a couple of tweaks I would do. And this is fine if you want to kind of give an overview of what you do. I typically do it a little bit differently. I typically want to take the focus immediately off me and put it on the prospect. So when I was in the trenches, like you, I didn't want to just start talking. I wanted the prospect to start talking.
I wanted the prospect to start telling me why they were possibly looking for what I had. Okay. So let's, and I don't know how this appointment was set up. I don't know if he had an SDR. And book this appointment. I don't know if he did this. It might be a role play. I have no idea. Okay. It's a good role play. It is because of what the prospect does. But let's say you're in this situation and an SDR call. Call, set up this appointment. You're the salesperson coming, or you did that, or let's just say an SDR did that. Okay. So I might start by saying, 'So, Hey, I know you were talking with one of my associates, Julie, the other day, when you guys were talking, what was it that you guys were kind of discussing that caused you to want to want to look into this further.
You know, want to want to look into this further. Now notice how neutral I am. I'm not saying that caused you to want to meet with me and talk about my solution. That's too assumptive early on.' If you're too assumptive early on without having trust and credibility here in the first minute, which you're probably not going to have, a lot of A-types will push back and they'll be like, 'Well, I'm just. Kind of looking around. I'm not saying I'm ready to buy. I'm just kind of looking at my options and the guards already met already up. Like you've already triggered sales resistance. So I'm more neutral. Yeah. So when you were talking. With Julie, what was it that you guys were kind of, I guess, going over that caused you to, hey, want to look into this further, see how neutral I am, see how my hands are raised like this, it affects my tonality.
Okay now, what I'm also doing when the prospect tells me what was it that they went over that caused them to, you know, want to have this conversation. You know, I'm going to word it differently. Not only are you finding out details, but more importantly, the prospect is reminding themselves on why they're even in this conversation, like why they've been booked at like problem. Why do they even have this conversation? Because what is the biggest, what is the number one thing that every prospect thinks about when a salesperson starts talking? You think about when a salesperson starts talking about what they offer to you? Most things you're going to think about is how much is this going to cost? So as a consumer, you know, you could be a business owner.
You could, you could be, you know, it doesn't matter. Every human being, when a salesperson first starts talking to them, the biggest thing they think about, even in the beginning, is how much is this going to cost? So that's called price or cost-based thinking. They have that frame. We want to take them out of that way of thinking, out of that frame. And we want to reframe into a new way of thinking, a new frame, which is results-based thinking. Okay. And I'm going to show you some techniques to do this, maybe even for your industry here in a minute. Let's keep going. This is getting good. I'm over the date. I even took some notes for you. In terms of growth, like, you know, stop you from growing as fast as you could.

Now, I like this, you know, let me ask you a question. I would just reword it a little bit because a lot of times, when you say, 'Let me ask you a question' or 'Let me ask you something' and you just ask it, a lot of because you feel like you're steamrolling them. Like you didn't give them, you didn't get their permission to ask that, especially A-type personalities. Sometimes they'll give you pushback. So a way to just tweak that and reword it and say, 'Can I? Can I ask you something?' That's simple. Okay, see the verbal posit. 'Can I ask you something?' They're always going to say yes. They're never going to say, 'No, you cannot ask me anything.' Lost my pen there.
So they're never going to say, 'No, you cannot ask me a question.' And it's almost like you're getting their permission, but it's impossible for them to say, 'No, you can't ask me anything.' It's just an easier way to do it. You want to have a little verbal pause. You want to say, 'Can I ask you a question?' Because that sounds too fast. It's too weird. It's like when you, the reason why prospects give you a lot of vague, generalized answers, surface-level answers, because you ask your questions too fast. So notice a verbal pause. Can I, can I ask you something? Yeah, what's going on? See how I lean in, like I'm like, 'It's a serious question.' Okay now, the only thing I would do differently, because when George says, 'I've been doing a lot of research on your industry,' the only red flag that could be in a prospect's brain is, 'Oh.' He doesn't really understand my industry.
Not necessarily even that. He doesn't really work. With companies in my industry, if he's doing research on companies in my industry, so some prospects that will trigger that thought process. So I want to eliminate that. So I was on your website looking up some info, you know. I was on your website the other day and you were talking about it. It's just a different way to reword it where you prevent an objection from happening. Where they're like, 'He doesn't understand my industry because he's just now doing research.' So just a little tweak there. Massive. Growth potential for you here domestically in Australia, right? You're really scratching the surface domestically. You have a good market share, but obviously the market's growing here pretty much 15, 16% a year.

And that companies like his, that's the biggest reason why they come to you. That means you have higher status. Companies are coming to you to solve this problem. So what that does in the prospect's brain is it causes them to feel like you have legitimacy, you're more credible.
That doesn't mean just because you do that one thing, your credibility goes through the roof, but at least it starts to raise, right? So it reduces the prospect's objections, the end saying, 'I need to do more research,' or 'can you send me references?' Like those objections go out the window when you see. Little things like that— as statements rather than saying, 'Oh well, don't worry, because we're the biggest in the market, and you'll never have that problem that doesn't see credibility— even if you are every Every salesperson says those types of things. So it lowers your credibility because you sound like everybody else. Okay. So I'm just going to reframe it and say, 'Well, the reason why I ask you is because that's actually the biggest reason why.
Companies in your space come to us is because they want access to our network. They want access to this and that to help them grow in these other countries. Would that help you if we were able to give you access to all that? Nobody's going to say no. Yeah. Well, how do you guys do that? And it triggers curiosity as well. Okay. In China, so one of the things we could do is actually introduce you to kind of various people along every element of the supply chain, whether it's simply purchasing an organic concern or simply getting you in touch with the right people. Operation save her a tremendous amount of money domestically and overseas on our commodities hedging, okay, and on some of our currency swaps that she was doing. And the reason we did that is because we understand... No, this is good. He's telling a story about another client that he has. I don't know if it's in this space or the space. He's telling the story of the struggle they had and why they came to him and how they saw him. It. I think that, you know, I don't know the full context behind this meeting, but I think that works. I would just always start that frame with, well, actually, the other day, you know, In fact, it brings up a company in your sector that came to us about a year ago, XYZ Company.
And the reason why they came to us is because of the same issue that you're having. Just frame it that way. The reason why they came to us. Okay. So it's like, you're getting the prospect to start to qualify to you because you're talking about all these other companies that are coming to you for this. Same issues that this prospect wants solved. That's why everybody else is doing. It's the herd theory in the brain. Okay. Social dynamics 101. If the herd is going this way, that must be a good thing. So I just start following the herd. That's the way most of our brains are wired to follow the herd. If they're going one way, we must think, we think it's good.
There are certain peculiarities to her industry, but at the same time, there were also some commonalities that applied to all industries. We were able to come up with a model that directly suited her needs, that would address... she has similar, not exact, similar concerns in the sense that she produces two a little different in a different industry, but she's producing not sure what she's going to sell. So it's important that she would, if I like, person I know. Over the long term, we can do a lot of stuff for you. But I think I can best serve you by just starting off doing one thing for you. Let me just start and try to do one thing for you really, really well. Now, what he's doing here is he's starting to frame.
And I've noticed that Jordan does that. Maybe it's just the Wolf on Wall Street show, or maybe that was fiction in the movie. He always talks about, like, 'Hey, let me do this one smaller thing, right?' Because it's an easier decision for the prospect. Smaller thing, less money, right? And let me prove my result. And then I'm going to get you for the bigger thing. Now you can use those techniques if you want. I typically start high and then, if they can't get the fun, I start to downsell low, okay? Because it seems like it's a better deal. Typically, I don't have to do that because I built a big enough gap when I was in the trenches, like you and four industries I sold in, from where— that prospect is to where they want to be, where they felt like, let's say if I was the second industry, I sold debt relief services.
I built such a big gap where they realized how much money they were losing in interest every month as a company. By paying the fee for us to take that over and refinance all that stuff, paying that fee was like pennies compared to all the money they would lose if they kept paying that amount of debt with that high of interest rate, if that makes sense. So it built such a big gap that it didn't matter what number I threw out. Emotionally involved at that point and triggered so much urgency that they didn't need to talk to anybody else, because they emotionally bonded. They emotionally i could attach their emotions to their problems and how the problems were like affecting their entire business in their entire lives, even if this is a huge company we're talking about.
Okay and I'll show you some techniques on that in a second. You'll want to stay here. Now, hey, if you have questions about this, like if you have questions about, hey, how would I do this? How would I do this for what I sell? Or, you know, the prospect. Told me the other day that I need to think it over. I did not resolve it. Or, you know, there's just an objection you're getting. You're wondering maybe how to prevent it. The easiest way to get ahold of me, because I rarely respond to comments on here, is simply to text me. Okay. So I'll give you my number just one time. It's 480-637-2944. Okay. So you're welcome to text me. And sometimes it takes a little while to answer your questions.
And either myself or one of our sales trainers, because they usually have sales trainers in a conference room, much bigger here. We sit around here for a few hours a day. We just respond to text messages on like six phones. So we will answer your question, right? So you're welcome to text me if you want to get your questions answered about selling. I don't want to interfere with what you do with Titan. I'm sure they're great. Everything I know of them is a very good, reputable company. Respect them. But we specified something a little bit different. One of the first things I loved... Now, I love the frame that he did here. I don't want to disrespect you with Titan. They're a great company, blah, blah, blah.
But now, when he says, 'But the only problem in the English language.' Use when you say something nice and then you say, but it basically negates everything you just said in that person's mind. So it'd be like if you're married and you're talking to your beautiful wife or husband or whatever. And you're like, 'Hey, honey, I really love your dress, but I just don't really like the way your shoes look.' She's not going to remember that you liked the dress at all. She's going to remember the thing you said you don't like, okay? Because it negates everything you just said. Hey, I totally agree with what you said. But here's my perspective. Well, no, you didn't agree with it. See, you negated what you just said.
So you want to be careful with the word, but, and I'm sure Jordan knows this. Sometimes I slip on that up when I was in sales, I'd say, but I'm like, shit, I can't believe I said that. I guarantee Jordan knows that. Now, instead of saying that the company you're with is a great company, I don't want to say they're a bad company either, because Jordan knows that would trigger resistance because it looks like you're biased. But I don't also want to say they're great. I want to be in the middle. Yeah, I mean, Titan that you've been with, I mean, they're fairly decent. I mean, you've been with them seven years. What's caused you to feel like they might not be able to get you. Really expanding into China, though.
Now see what I just did there, okay? It's very subtle with my tone, okay? The words fairly decent, but the way I said them with my tone signifies what? That I might know something about them that's not that good. Because nobody wants something fairly decent, right? Now, listen to my tongue. Yeah, I mean, Titan, you've been with them for seven years. I mean, they're fairly decent and firm. I mean... See, see how my tone starts to show doubt that I know something that's not that good. So I'm automatically seeing doubt, but I'm not triggering the prospect to get defensive and say. Oh no, they're great. They're not going to say that because I'm neutral in the middle, but my tone signifies that I do know something that might not be so good.
And then I'm going to wrap that question around. So rather than asking, 'Hey, what are some challenges that you have? Or what are some problems you have?' Because most prospects know where that script leads to. They know where that question leads to, right? know that you're trying to extract problems. And that's why they don't tell you a lot. They give you surface-level answers. So I want them to defend themselves on why they might be looking to change. So I'm going to reframe that question and say, 'So you've been with Titan the last seven years. I mean, they're fairly decent. I mean, what's caused you to feel like... You might want to look at someone else. Oh, don't get us wrong.
We like Titan, but, and then they start telling you things they don't like because I got them to defend themselves on why they might be looking. Notice it said 'my'— because I haven't built a big enough gap yet to assume yet still kind of neutral, but it gets them to defend themselves on why they might be looking for somebody else like Jordan, see what I'm saying there. See, I'm doing that, okay? To try is to take a look at exactly what you're doing with your commodities, the hedging strategies, and the currency stuff. Which is something you're doing both in Singapore... Hong Kong and China. Okay. And I want to get in there right away, straight away. And I want to go in there and give me a shot to beat it.
Let me work. Give me 30 days. I'll do my trick small. Start small. Don't stop what you're doing. Put a small amount of your trades through me, and then analyze in 30 days the percentage gain. And I'm even half as good as to say, I believe you'll be moving all your business over to me. After that. That sound fair enough? Well, I mean, I'm not particularly worried about where we're at in that space. I mean, what particularly do we want to be doing? Yeah. Do you see how he triggered the prospect to react that way? And I know Jordan, if he had more time, probably would have built a bigger gap, right? He's only got 20 minutes. Okay. So the problem is he didn't.
In my mind, build a big enough gap yet to go in for that soft close. So what he's doing, he's like doing like a soft close. Like, is that fair enough? Okay. After 10 minutes, he's like, let me start small. I'll prove myself, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. I think that's good. I would just. I would build a bigger gap before I tried to close here. I might go three, four minutes and actually build a gap from where that prospect is to where they want to be. Because so far I haven't heard much pain from the prospect. I've heard more talking between both of them, but the prospect so far, in, like, I said, maybe this is just a role play or something. I'm not sure. So I'll give Jordan the benefit of the doubt.
He's obviously was a great salesperson, you know, back in the eighties and nineties and all that stuff. So obviously knows what he was doing. But there's no gap here. So the prospect automatically is true. He's like, well, you know, I'm not necessarily ready. I don't think we were doing that bad in that part. So then, objection happens right there. I want to prevent that objection from happening. I want to build a bigger gap. Less talking, more questions, and getting the prospect to relive their pain. So going into China, though, because a lot of companies come to us for that. That's a big reason why they want to work with us. Going into that market, why? Why so important to you now, though? See, that's a probing question.
Now, I'm not going to ask that in the first two minutes, but I might ask that five, six, seven minutes in. And I have that verbal positive. Why so important to you guys now, though? See that little verbal pause right before, now though. Now, where did I force the prospect's brain to think about? Why is it important now? Like the timing, why now? Triggers what? More urgency for them to change their situation because obviously they would never have this meeting if they felt that they had the right company that could get them in there or the right broker, whatever they're selling. I don't know the context here. Well, let me ask you a question. First of all, does the overall idea, in terms of, you know, of us advising you, not just in one area, but all areas, make sense to you that it could be an asset over the loan?
Does that appeal to you? Well, I guess it certainly does. It just depends on, you know, what it's going to cost us. See what he's framing there. Like the overall strategy of this and this, does that appeal to you? And the prospect said, 'Yes,' but then they say what? Well, it depends on how much it's going to cost. Now, here's where I would do this a lot differently. In the very beginning of the conversation, remember I told you: What is the biggest way of thinking any human being thinks when they first start talking to a salesperson? Every human being thinks, 'How much is it going to cost me?' When a salesperson approaches you, you, one of the first things you start to think about, you might not ask it— is how much is this going to cost?
Can I afford it? You're instantly thinking that. That is called cost-based thinking or a cost-based frame. So in the very beginning, I want to ask questions that take the prospect out of that way of thinking, that frame of cost or price, and I want to reframe them. I want to deframe them. out of that way of thinking it's called a d frame that's for a whole nother video look if you want to know about deframing how do you deframe a prospect out of a a way of thinking so you prevent objections Text me. I can tell you that. It's 480-637-2944. I'll explain that to you. I'm going to deframe them out of that way of thinking, and I'm going to reframe them into a new way of thinking.
results-based thinking. So at the very beginning, I might have said, okay, so yeah, you were talking with my assistant, Julie. She said that you asked us to come in to kind of go over maybe different ways on how we could possibly help you get into more of the Chinese market so you could really grow and scale the company there, right? If that's what I was selling, so let's say, if I'm selling business consulting, I just gave an example of that. So you can grow and scale your business in that market. Right. So, if I'm selling business consulting, what's the result of that the business grows and scales. That would be the result of business consulting. Okay.
If he's talking about, say, I don't know what this company does here, I don't know the context, but if it's financial, then you can, you know, grow and, you know, to get into the Chinese markets, you really grow your company's, your profit margins or grow your profit or grow your company's revenue. Or, you know, your company's net worth that they're investing in or whatever that is. Like I said, I don't know the full context. But right there, those type of questions start to frame, get the prospect out. Price or cost-based thinking and frame them into results-based thinking, the end result of what you're offering, okay? Not the thing. I always say you're not selling the thing. You're not selling your product. Product or service, you're selling the results of what your thing does, okay?
That's results-based thinking. So you get way less of how much is this going to cost me because now I've got their brain to focus on the end result of where they want to be. Make sense? Exactly. And of course, cost always in the end comes down to it, but it's not just so much cost, it's also the value you get from the cost, right? But one of the things we pride ourselves on... No, that was a good one. It's not about... It always comes down to the price at the end. And it also comes down to the value of the cost or whatever. That's a good frame that he put him in because that makes sense. Yeah, that makes sense. So that was a good frame there that he did there.
I would do it a little bit different. I would say, yeah, it just depends on, you know, what a company views more expensive. I mean, is it more expensive to get the funding together and we're able to help you get into the, you know. Into China and you guys are able to, let's say, we only help you get a 20% return on your money, or is it more expensive for you guys not to even get into the market? Stay at, you know, the 6% you're getting here in Australia. If that's what he said, see, I don't know the context. I'm just, that's a comparative frame. And then that causes the possibility, like, look, oh yeah, it's, it's more expensive if we just stay at 6%.
Than if we're able to get into this other market and go to 20%. That's way better for us. It's more expensive if I don't do anything. So I'm just reframing them back into results-based thinking. Just a different tweak I would do myself. Just so you know, as a company, the true beauty of what we do is that every transaction that we do here, we're looking always at two things. A, to keep costs down as much as possible. A, brokerage, hedging. helping you purchase an asset you didn't think you could afford, helping you expand into a new market, going public perhaps, okay, getting a bridge loan in a foreign market. Of course, we always look at cost, okay? But we also look at three to five years.
Down the road, okay, because sometimes the cheapest money is not the best money. So of course, we'll lay both options out. Hold on, are you going to make fun of our website? And I'm playful, like, no, I would never make fun of your website. And so now they start to laugh, their guard comes down, and then I immediately go more of a soft, curious tone, just like, 'Yeah, what would you like to know about the company?' And this, hey, this is probably not an easy prospect, right? But now he's making it on the defensive. Where he's like, 'Well, let me reinduce myself. My name's Jordan Belfort. I'm a salesperson and we pride ourselves on.' I think that's good. And Jordan's really good at talking. And so he can like overcome that resistance. And still close, a lot of prospects or a lot of sales people have a hard time doing that. They have a hard time being on the defensive and then just explaining it through just talking. And trying to close in by just persuading by talking. For most of those people, that's hard to do. So an easier way to do this is like I just said, well, yeah, I mean, I can give you our. website.
Now, hold on. You're going to make fun of our website if I show it to you. We're not that cool. Oh, I'm sure you guys are cool. I just want to know about it. Well, yeah, in all seriousness, what would you like to know about the company? Well, I'd like to know about this. Okay, well, in this example, here's what we do. And now I know what he wants to know, and I'm answering the questions, and I'm overcoming the concerns. And now who's in control of the conversation? Me. When I'm talking, trying to explain and sell, the prospect is like the puppeteer and you're like the little puppet and they're making you do a little dance. Now, Jordan is really, really good at doing that dance and still overcoming it. 99.
9% of salespeople are not, okay? Because they're just not smooth talkers like that. So if you just reframe that and ask that question, now you're making the prospect do the dance and you're in the one control of the conversation. That's a tweak I would do myself. I'm one of the top salesmen at the company, and I am a salesman, by the way. I pride myself on actually going out there and, you know, going after the people that I know I can help, okay? But one thing I do do is I put my clients' needs above everything else. I work literally around the clock, tirelessly. I'm not an analyst. I'm not an investment banker. Okay. But I literally will go. One thing Jordan has, he has really good certainty.
So the prospect reads the tone and the body language that he's certain he can get you that result. I love that. That's one thing I would say that Jordan's really, really talented at is the certainty. And the tone is certain. Okay. I would just say, keep the certain tone. Just don't go in defensive mode where you're having to explain everything. Just reframe it. Okay. You know, kind of almost, you know, in a playful tone. Well, yeah. Oh yeah. I can give you our website and everything. Now, hold on. You're not, you're not going to think our website's not that cool. You're not going to make fun of us. Are you? Oh, I would never make fun of you.
They don't say, 'Yeah, I'm going to make fun of you.' They're like, 'No, I would never make fun.' This is a playful tone. And then I just lean and say, 'What would you like to know about the company?' And now I'm in control. So much easier, so much quicker. It's just a different frame I would use. Add it, like I'm a researcher of these people and I would find the absolute best person. Because typically, how many salespeople say, 'Yeah, you don't have to worry about it.' I'm the number one salesperson. I work the hardest. I do this, I do that. Prospects have heard that before. How many salespeople sell them anything, say they're the best, they're going to work the hardest for them, they're going to give them the best result.
It's like it's almost in one ear, out the other. And Jordan can get away with it because he has a lot more certainty than most salespeople do. I'm just saying there's easier things to do where you don't have to deal with all the sales resistance. And that's why he closed a lot. Back in the 80s and 90s. I would say prospects are a lot different. They're more skeptical. There's far more advertising and marketing in their face with social media and the internet now than ever been before. I call it the post-trust era. If you've ever read my book, it's true. Trust has definitely gone down since the 80s and 90s. I can tell you that for sure. It wasn't like it was easy to sell in the 80s or 90s. I'm not making that assumption. It wasn't. But prospects are far more skeptical now. So if you sound like everybody else, their guard just goes up. Okay. So just easier ways to do that in my mind. Our business is around commodities, and from there we went back as a result of the hedging.
So that's why we're the number one company about hedging and commodities, which is why it used to be perfect. So instead of saying that's why we're the number one company hedging, just say, that's probably the, I mean, that's the biggest reason why companies in your sector tell us they came to us is because of the hedging. See, it's like third-party validation rather than you saying you're number one. Because everybody says it's like it's like me saying we're the number one sales training. I'm the number one sales trainer. Well, according to who, right? That's why I said we are the largest business-to-consumer sales training company based on annual revenue. You can look that up on Selling Power Magazine. It's just a ranking you have to submit. Your tax returns and your PLs to prove it, right?
If I just said, we're the number one sales training company in the world, you're like, 'Yeah, whatever, dude.' Everybody says that. Grandma will tell you that, right? Want to have third-party validation. Okay. Yeah. That's probably the biggest, I mean, the biggest reason why our clients tell us they come to us is because we're the, is because of our hedging and this and that, that's far more credible than say. We're the number one at this. Even if you are, your prospects don't necessarily believe that because every salesperson selling them anything says that. How many salespeople you talk to that says we're the 17th best in the market? Nobody. Everybody says they're number one. Just simply in terms of me, you'll only be dealing with me.
And if you have to, if there's someone else I want you to meet, they'll be with me. In other words, if I say I've got a guy who has a new algorithm that's going to allow us to make an extra seven basis points a month on the term. I'm not going to say, 'Hey, you guys come with me.' I'll have them come with me or we can have dinner or whatever it might be, but it's not going to be 30 faces. It is going to be me and only someone else if I believe that person, okay, can help with the conversation. If you only want to meet me, hey, you know, you can just be the two of us, but again, all I'm asking for is this.
I'm asking to get married right now, okay? I believe in starting small. Because I know I can prove myself. Now, this is a good frame that he does. And, you know, I was doing a keynote. There were two keynote speakers at an event that I was just at a couple weeks ago in Gold Coast, Australia, myself and Daniel. G. Loved Andrew G.'s first time I met him. I've spoken at different events with him, but just on different days. But I actually got to meet him. And he talks about selling the exit. You're always selling the exit. You're about to leave because it reduces the sales pressure. It lowers the sales resistance. Now, at my company, we call it 'you're disarming the prospect.' Daniel G.
calls it 'you're selling the exit.' You're always about to leave. I'm disarming them. I say, 'Oh, and I apologize.' I've only got a few minutes before my next appointment. See, I'm disarming them because they feel like I'm not going to take that much time and try to close them, right? Now, they're not going to come back a few minutes there and say, 'You just said you have two minutes.' They don't because I know how to emotionally get them involved. That's what we train you to do.
